summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the primary energy source that drives photosynthesis in most terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems?

Sunlight provides the electromagnetic radiation (primarily in the 400–700 nm visible spectrum) that photosynthetic organisms absorb using pigments like chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotenoids. This light energy excites electrons in photosystem II, initiating the light-dependent reactions that ultimately produce ATP and NADPH for the Calvin cycle, making solar radiation the foundational energy input for most ecosystems.

A physiologist measures the metabolic rate of a black bear (Ursus americanus) during winter hibernation and finds it has decreased to approximately 25% of its active summer rate. What is the bear's primary energy source during this prolonged dormancy?

During hibernation, bears rely on massive fat reserves accumulated during hyperphagic fall feeding. Triglycerides stored in adipose tissue are mobilized through lipolysis, and the resulting fatty acids undergo beta-oxidation in the mitochondria to produce acetyl-CoA, which enters the citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation. Fat yields approximately 9 kcal/gram (compared to 4 kcal/gram for carbohydrates), making it the ideal long-term energy storage molecule.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: According to the 10% rule in ecology, approximately what percentage of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next in a food chain?

Raymond Lindeman's 10% rule (1942) states that on average only about 10% of the energy stored in one trophic level is converted into biomass at the next trophic level. The remaining ~90% is lost primarily as metabolic heat through cellular respiration, with smaller amounts lost in undigested food, urine, and feces. This explains why food chains rarely exceed 4–5 trophic levels.

Example: Marine scientists study a coral reef and discover that coral polyps (animals) host photosynthetic dinoflagellate algae called zooxanthellae within their tissues. The algae provide up to 90% of the coral's energy needs. What ecological relationship is this, and how does it relate to energy acquisition?

This is obligate mutualism: zooxanthellae (genus Symbiodinium) perform photosynthesis within coral gastrodermal cells, translocating up to 90% of their photosynthate (glucose, glycerol, amino acids) to the coral host. In return, corals provide zooxanthellae with CO₂, nitrogen, phosphorus, and a stable, sunlit habitat. When stressed by elevated sea temperatures, corals expel their zooxanthellae (coral bleaching), losing their primary energy source and often dying if the stress persists.
